For git URLs, this is probably the right behavior (git doesn't distinguish), but for operators auditing manifests the distinction is lost. Separately, `https://github.com/foo/bar%00.git` (null byte in path) is rejected by net/url.Parse with 'invalid control character in URL' -- good. Newline (`\\x0a`) and tab in path also rejected. But embedded `\\xff` (non-UTF-8 byte) in path is accepted by url.Parse and becomes `%FF` after canonicalization. No rejection of arbitrary 8-bit bytes in path.",
185	      "impact": "Low. Any two URLs that differ only in their %-encoding of path characters converge to one cache key, which is arguably correct. The non-UTF-8 byte case is more concerning: `https://example.com/\\xff/x` canonicalizes to `https://example.com/%FF/x.git` and hashes successfully. A git clone will likely fail, but the cache directory gets created. No security impact (hash truncates to hex), but another form of audit-file pollution if an adversarial manifest contains 8-bit-binary in canonical.url.",
186	      "recommendation": "Require canonical.url to be valid ASCII + printable UTF-8 (reject bytes outside the printable ASCII range + Unicode letters). Or accept them and document the canonicalization's %-encoding behavior so audit readers aren't surprised.",
187	      "owner": "human",
188	      "autofix_class": "advisory"
189	    },
190	    {
191	      "id": "adv-014",
192	      "title": "Abuse: IPv6 bracket-host with default port silently becomes unbracketed in canonical form",
193	      "severity": "medium",
194	      "confidence": 0.90,
195	      "category": "assumption-violation",
196	      "file": "internal/cache/canonicalize.go",
197	      "lines": "77-85",
198	      "evidence_constructed_scenario": "Input `https://[::1]:443/foo/bar`. `u.Hostname()` returns `::1` (no brackets). The code then sets `u.Host = host` (no brackets, just `::1`) because 443 is the default port for https and is stripped. u.String() produces `https://::1/foo/bar.git`. Verified. This string is not a valid URL by RFC 3986 (IPv6 literals must be bracketed), and re-parsing it would fail. A canonical form that doesn't round-trip through url.Parse is a latent bug for any downstream tool that re-parses. Also, for the NON-default-port case `https://[::1]:8080/foo/bar`, line 82 does `host + \":\" + port` which produces `::1:8080` (again unbracketed), also invalid.",
199	      "impact": "Any IPv6-host Git URL (rare for public repos but common for internal dev infra) canonicalizes to a form that re-parse cannot handle. Audit file records an invalid URL. If any downstream consumer (Unit 5's git gateway, Unit 6's trust-store) treats canonical URL as re-parseable, it will fail. Test coverage includes no IPv6 case, so this is invisible until deployment.",
200	      "recommendation": "Rebracket IPv6 hostnames when assigning u.Host: if host contains ':' it's a literal IPv6 address and must be wrapped in brackets in u.Host. Fix: `if strings.Contains(host, \":\") { host = \"[\" + host + \"]\" }` before the u.Host assignment. Add golden fixtures: `https://[::1]/foo/bar` -> `https://[::1]/foo/bar.git`; `https://[::1]:443/foo/bar` -> `https://[::1]/foo/bar.git`; `https://[::1]:8080/foo/bar` -> `https://[::1]:8080/foo/bar.git`.",
201	      "owner": "human",
202	      "autofix_class": "advisory"
203	    },
204	    {
205	      "id": "adv-015",
206	      "title": "Cascade: WriteAudit overwrite on a shared cache key allows one workspace to clobber another's audit record",
207	      "severity": "medium",
208	      "confidence": 0.80,
209	      "category": "shared-state-mutation",
210	      "file": "internal/cache/location.go",
211	      "lines": "94-102",
212	      "evidence_constructed_scenario": "Two workspaces W1 and W2 on the same machine both resolve the same canonical URL (U1). Both compute the same key K = Key(U1). Both Resolve() to the same Dir and AuditPath. W1 calls WriteAudit() with its Location{Canonical: U1_W1_copy}; W2 then calls WriteAudit() with Location{Canonical: U1_W2_copy}. Since the audit path is shared and WriteAudit uses os.WriteFile with O_TRUNC, W2's content overwrites W1's. If W1 and W2 came from distinct raw URLs that canonicalized identically, their audit files differ only in the raw form (which isn't stored). But: an adversarial workspace that holds a shared cache root (e.g., `/opt/aienvs-cache` passed as Override via both manifests) can craft a Location manually with a DIFFERENT Canonical than the key's real pre-image and call WriteAudit() -- overwriting the audit record for a victim workspace's key with a lie. Verified via test that constructs a Location with a forged Canonical and WriteAudit overwrites successfully.",
213	      "impact": "For a shared-cache scenario (e.g., a build agent running many repo syncs under one cache root), audit integrity is not preserved. An incident response team looking at `canonical-url.txt` after a suspicious sync could be misled about what URL the cache entry corresponds to. Mitigating factor: the cache key SHA is irreversible, so the audit file is the ONLY human-readable source of truth -- which makes its forgeability a real problem. The plan's statement 'safe to write into an audit trail (no secrets)' is true but the audit trail itself is not integrity-protected.",
214	      "recommendation": "Either (a) require WriteAudit to re-verify that Location.Canonical hashes to Location.Key before writing (cheap, 1 line), (b) use O_EXCL|O_CREATE and tolerate 'already exists' as success-no-op when content matches (read-verify-skip), or (c) document explicitly that audit is best-effort and not an integrity record. (a) is the strongest: it prevents forged-Canonical writes outright.",
215	      "owner": "human",
216	      "autofix_class": "advisory"
217	    }
218	  ],
219	  "residual_risks": [
220	    {
221	      "id": "res-001",
222	      "description": "Unit 12 per-external-file flock assumes every adapter uses it for shared tool-owned files. Nothing in Units 9/10/11 scope description enforces that an adapter MUST acquire the flock before write_tool_owned. An adapter that skips the lock breaks the serialization guarantee silently.",
223	      "surface": "internal/adapter/*"
224	    },
225	    {
226	      "id": "res-002",
227	      "description": "manifest.LoadFile does not size-cap input (separately flagged by reliability reviewer rel-003). In the adversarial angle, a 10MB manifest with 9.9MB of ref string loads in ~160ms. A CI job where .aienv.yaml is populated by a user-controlled generator could produce adversarially large manifests.",
228	      "surface": "internal/manifest"
229	    },
230	    {
231	      "id": "res-003",
232	      "description": "Plan contradicts itself on MVP Unit 14 inclusion; whichever resolution is picked, the mid-life drift guard (decision #12 'ledger-or-adopt holds for the entire lifetime') will land later than Unit 13 and there's a window in between where the invariant is claimed but not enforced.",
233	      "surface": "docs/plans"
234	    },
235	    {
236	      "id": "res-004",
237	      "description": "Canonicalize path-normalization only handles '/' and '.git'. It does NOT reject dot-segments ('../', './') or homoglyph unicode in path. Downstream consumers that interpret canonical URL as a git-remote URL for clone will error; consumers that use it as a display string may mislead users.",
238	      "surface": "internal/cache/canonicalize.go"
239	    }
240	  ],
241	  "testing_gaps": [
242	    {
243	      "id": "tg-001",
244	      "description": "No canonicalize test covers IPv6 literal hosts (both with and without non-default port). Missing fixture will let adv-014 regress.",
245	      "suggested_test": "Add to testdata/urls/golden.txt: `https://[::1]/foo/bar` -> `https://[::1]/foo/bar.git`; `https://[::1]:443/foo/bar` -> `https://[::1]/foo/bar.git`; `https://[::1]:8080/foo/bar` -> `https://[::1]:8080/foo/bar.git`."
246	    },
247	    {
248	      "id": "tg-002",
249	      "description": "No manifest write test covers CRLF input. adv-002's silent line-ending conversion is invisible.",
250	      "suggested_test": "Add testdata/manifest/commented-manifest-crlf.yaml (same content as commented-manifest.yaml but with \\r\\n line terminators) and assert that WriteResolvedSHA either preserves CRLF or explicitly rejects CRLF input."
251	    },
252	    {
253	      "id": "tg-003",
254	      "description": "No manifest write test covers inline (same-line) comments on the target scalars. adv-003 is invisible.",
255	      "suggested_test": "Add a fixture with `commit: \"\" # placeholder` and `trusted_sha: \"\" # fill me`; assert behavior (preserved or explicitly rejected)."
256	    },
257	    {
258	      "id": "tg-004",
259	      "description": "Discovery tests do not exercise StopAt being outside the cwd ancestor chain, nor the 'manifest is a directory' case.",
260	      "suggested_test": "Add TestFind_StopAtOutsideAncestorChain and TestFind_ManifestIsDirectoryShadowedByAncestor. Both fail today per adv-007 and adv-008."
261	    },
262	    {
263	      "id": "tg-005",
264	      "description": "No test asserts that after Validate(), m.Canonical.Commit/m.TrustedSHA contain trimmed values. The TrimSpace happens in a local variable only.",
265	      "suggested_test": "Add a load test: give manifest with ` commit: \"1111...1111 \" `, assert err=nil AND m.Canonical.Commit == \"1111...1111\" (no whitespace). Fails today per adv-001."
266	    },
267	    {
268	      "id": "tg-006",
269	      "description": "No test verifies WriteAudit integrity (that Canonical hashes to Key).",
270	      "suggested_test": "Construct Location with Canonical=X and Key=Key(Y) where X!=Y; call WriteAudit; assert either error or that the written content matches what Canonicalize(X) would produce (not a forged string)."
271	    }
272	  ]
273	}

file.content:
{
  "reviewer": "adversarial",
  "run_id": "20260421-131203-a748e9ab",
  "base_sha": "b13002e5f07489b2c24589dfc0cfba7b252afe1b",
  "scope": "internal/manifest, internal/workspace, internal/cache, docs/plans (Unit 8b/12a additions)",
  "depth": "deep",
  "findings": [
    {
      "id": "adv-001",
      "title": "Composition: whitespace in canonical.commit passes validation but propagates to Git clone arg",
      "severity": "high",
      "confidence": 0.92,
      "category": "assumption-violation-composition",
      "file": "internal/manifest/load.go",
      "lines": "80-97",
      "evidence_constructed_scenario": "Adversarial manifest: `commit: \" 1111...1111 \"` (40 hex + leading/trailing spaces, 42 chars). Validate() does `commit := strings.TrimSpace(m.Canonical.Commit)` then checks regex on the TRIMMED copy. Regex passes. But the struct field `m.Canonical.Commit` is NEVER rewritten with the trimmed value -- Go's yaml decoder stores the quoted scalar verbatim. Caller reads `m.Canonical.Commit` and sees ` 1111...1111 ` (len=42). Verified by running `manifest.LoadBytes` on a file with `commit: \" 1111111111111111111111111111111111111111 \"` -- load returns err=nil, m.Canonical.Commit = \" 1111111111111111111111111111111111111111 \" len=42. Second vector: asymmetric whitespace where `commit: \" 1111...1111\"` (leading space) and `trusted_sha: 1111...1111` (bare) both TrimSpace-equal -- Validate accepts (trimmed commit == trimmed trusted_sha), but struct fields differ. Downstream comparison `m.Canonical.Commit == m.TrustedSHA` fails even though Validate said they matched. Downstream consumers (unit 5 git clone, unit 6 trust-verify, Key() on a SHA-parameterized cache key) will either (a) pass whitespace-laden SHA to `git checkout <sha>` (git will reject but only after the clone transport opens), or (b) silently compare unequal and re-emit.",
      "impact": "Sync or trust-verify reports an inconsistency where the loader said things are consistent. Error messages will point at the wrong layer. Worst case: the cached clone is materialized (network cost incurred) and then `git checkout` fails on the whitespace; user sees an opaque git error.",
      "recommendation": "Validate should rewrite the trimmed values back onto the struct (`m.Canonical.Commit = commit; m.TrustedSHA = trusted`) after the regex check passes. Or, stricter: reject any commit/trusted_sha containing whitespace, reserving the canonical form for byte-exact 40-hex. Pick one and document it on schema.go. Updating the spec at docs/spec/manifest-v1.md to note \"whitespace in SHA values is normalized / rejected\" closes the surprise.",
      "owner": "human",
      "autofix_class": "advisory"
    },
    {
      "id": "adv-002",
      "title": "Cascade: WriteResolvedSHA silently converts CRLF manifests to LF",
      "severity": "high",
      "confidence": 0.95,
      "category": "composition-silent-structural-change",
      "file": "internal/manifest/write.go",
      "lines": "56-78",
      "evidence_constructed_scenario": "Input bytes `\"version: 1\\r\\ncanonical:\\r\\n  url: https://example.com/x.git\\r\\n  commit: \\\"\\\"\\r\\ntrusted_sha: \\\"\\\"\\r\\n\"` (CRLF line endings, as produced by any Windows editor or a Git repo with `core.autocrlf=true`). Calling `WriteResolvedSHA(input, \"1111...\", \"\")` returns LF-only bytes: `bytes.Contains(before, []byte(\"\\r\\n\"))` is true; `bytes.Contains(after, []byte(\"\\r\\n\"))` is false. goccy's AST `file.String()` serializer emits LF unconditionally; the parser/AST does not round-trip the input's line terminator. Test verified this behavior directly against /Users/justinbeaudry/Projects/aienvs/internal/manifest/write.go#WriteResolvedSHA.",
      "impact": "On Windows / CRLF-configured workspaces, `aienvs init` or `aienvs trust pin` silently rewrites the entire manifest's line endings. Consequences: (a) Git reports the whole file as modified on every sync (diff noise), (b) if `.gitattributes` pins the file to CRLF, Git's next checkout will re-convert, creating a reverse diff at every commit -- a ping-pong with the user's editor, (c) a PR diff review for what should be a two-character SHA change shows every line modified, (d) code-owner auto-assigners fire on the wrong people. The plan's 'comments and key order are preserved byte-for-byte' contract (write.go:32 docstring) is broken in practice for any non-LF input.",
      "recommendation": "Detect input line terminator before parse (first `\\r\\n` vs first `\\n` wins), normalize to LF for parsing, then post-process the `file.String()` output to match. Or: reject CRLF input at the top of WriteResolvedSHA with a clean error and rely on the CLI to normalize at ingest. Update docs/spec/manifest-v1.md to document the chosen behavior. Add a CRLF fixture to testdata/manifest/ and a round-trip test.",
      "owner": "human",
      "autofix_class": "advisory"
    },
    {
      "id": "adv-003",
      "title": "Cascade: WriteResolvedSHA loses inline (same-line) comments",
      "severity": "high",
      "confidence": 0.95,
      "category": "contract-violation",
      "file": "internal/manifest/write.go",
      "lines": "32-40, 110-125",
      "evidence_constructed_scenario": "Input: `commit: \"\" # placeholder` and `trusted_sha: \"\" # please fill in`. After `WriteResolvedSHA(orig, sha, sha)`, output line is `commit: <sha>` -- the `# placeholder` trailing comment is gone. Same for trusted_sha. Verified by running WriteResolvedSHA on an input with inline comments and checking the bytes. Head-of-line comments (above the key) ARE preserved (tested by existing `TestWriteResolvedSHA_PreservesComments`), but the same-line trailing comment on the target scalar is dropped because goccy's `ReplaceWithReader` on the scalar node replaces the node's text including the trailing comment.",
      "impact": "The init template is likely to use inline comments (`commit: \"\" # filled by aienvs init`) because that's where the explanation reads best. Users adopting aienvs run `aienvs init`, and the explanatory comment they wrote next to the empty placeholder disappears silently. The docstring at write.go:32 claims 'every comment and key outside the targeted nodes is preserved byte-for-byte' -- this is false for inline comments on the target line. The plan's commented-manifest fixture only uses head comments, masking the bug in the existing test suite.",
      "recommendation": "Either (a) make WriteResolvedSHA preserve inline comments on the replaced scalar (extract the comment node before ReplaceWithReader and re-attach), (b) explicitly document 'inline comments on canonical.commit and trusted_sha are dropped; put explanatory comments on the line above' and enforce that the init template uses head comments, or (c) add a pre-write check that refuses the write if inline comments exist on the target lines. Pick (a) if achievable with goccy; else (b) + update docs/spec/manifest-v1.md Writing section. Add a fixture with inline comments to lock behavior.",
      "owner": "human",
      "autofix_class": "advisory"
    },
    {
      "id": "adv-004",
      "title": "Abuse: Canonicalize accepts URL with no path and produces a non-repo canonical form",
      "severity": "medium",
      "confidence": 0.90,
      "category": "assumption-violation",
      "file": "internal/cache/canonicalize.go",
      "lines": "145-158",
      "evidence_constructed_scenario": "Input `https://github.com` (no path component, no trailing slash). `url.Parse` succeeds, u.Path == \"\", normalizeGitPath returns \"\" unchanged (first branch `if p == \"\" || p == \"/\" { return p }`), so .git is NOT appended. Output: `https://github.com` with no path at all. Verified directly: `cache.ResolveFromRaw(\"https://github.com\", ...)` returns a valid Location with key `86baee67...`. This input is not a repository; yet Canonicalize produces a stable-looking canonical form and the pipeline will happily try to materialize `git clone https://github.com` into the cache. Second vector: `https://github.com/` (trailing slash only, path==\"/\") -- normalizeGitPath keeps it as `/` so output is `https://github.com/` with a distinct key. So `https://github.com` and `https://github.com/` map to distinct cache directories, both nonsensical.",
      "impact": "A typo during `aienvs init --url https://github.com` silently produces a cache entry instead of rejecting the URL. Unit 5's git clone will fail, but only after the cache directory and audit file exist. Operator diagnosing the failure sees a cache entry with no content, no clear error. Key-space pollution is permanent for that SHA unless cleaned. Separate collision concern: `https://github.com` key != `https://github.com/` key, so two users who mean the same thing but type them differently (one with trailing slash) hit different cache slots even after `aienvs init` has normalized elsewhere.",
      "recommendation": "Require at least one non-empty path segment after the host; reject empty-path URLs with ErrUnsupportedURL. Add a unit test to golden fixtures asserting rejection. This also closes the `https://github.com` vs `https://github.com/` collision risk.",
      "owner": "human",
      "autofix_class": "advisory"
    },
    {
      "id": "adv-005",
      "title": "Abuse: trailing .git normalization produces absurd canonical paths for .gitlab / .gitignore / etc",
      "severity": "medium",
      "confidence": 0.88,
      "category": "rule-boundary-walking",
      "file": "internal/cache/canonicalize.go",
      "lines": "153-156",
      "evidence_constructed_scenario": "normalizeGitPath: `if !strings.HasSuffix(p, \".git\") { p += \".git\" }`. Adversarial inputs verified via local harness: `https://host.example/foo.gitlab` -> `https://host.example/foo.gitlab.git`; `https://host.example/foo/bar.gitignore` -> `https://host.example/foo/bar.gitignore.git`; `git@host:.` -> `ssh://git@host/..git` (path = `..git`, aka 'dot-dot-git'); `https://github.com/Foo/Bar/.git` -> `https://github.com/Foo/Bar/.git` (already-.git suffix so left alone, but `.git` as the filename component is a literal git-hidden-dir name, not a repo name). The rule checks string suffix, not path-component suffix. The `https://github.com/foo/bar.git.git` -> `https://github.com/foo/bar.git.git` case is left alone (already .git suffix), but now a user who accidentally typed `.git.git` gets a cache key distinct from `foo/bar.git`.",
      "impact": "Two vectors. (1) Keyspace pollution: typos produce stable, distinct, non-obvious cache keys that will never be hit by the correct URL but consume disk indefinitely (no cleanup). (2) Collision inversion: a canonical form ending in `..git` (constructed from `git@host:.`) is not parseable as a valid git remote and will error at clone time with a confusing message -- but the canonicalize stage already committed to the keyspace. The rule 'suffix .git if missing' is intuitive for well-formed inputs but has no defensive check for suffix being a valid-looking repo-name suffix.",
      "recommendation": "Apply the .git suffix only to the last path component's final segment when the component does not already look like an extension (`^[^.]+$` or extension is the literal `.git`). Reject `..git` and path components that are just `.git`. Extend the golden fixture set with these adversarial pairs, asserting either a canonical reject or a normalized-to-reject outcome.",
      "owner": "human",
      "autofix_class": "advisory"
    },
    {
      "id": "adv-006",
      "title": "Abuse: scp-style regex accepts path containing colons, breaking the URL parse that follows",
      "severity": "medium",
      "confidence": 0.85,
      "category": "contract-mismatch",
      "file": "internal/cache/canonicalize.go",
      "lines": "106-123",
      "evidence_constructed_scenario": "scpPattern = `^([A-Za-z0-9._-]+)@([A-Za-z0-9.-]+):([^:].*)$`. The path group `[^:].*` requires the FIRST byte to not be a colon, but subsequent bytes can be anything. Input `git@host:foo:bar/x` matches: path = `foo:bar/x`. scpToSSH produces `ssh://git@host/foo:bar/x`. url.Parse then happily parses this, and u.Path becomes `/foo:bar/x`. normalizeGitPath appends `.git`: `ssh://git@host/foo:bar/x.git`. Verified directly. Consequences: the canonical form contains a colon in the path, which is unusual for Git URLs. More importantly, a hostile (or merely confused) user pasting `git@host:../../../etc/passwd` also matches the scp pattern -> `ssh://git@host/../../../etc/passwd.git`, and this canonical form goes into a SHA hash that will be used as a directory name under the cache root. So the path traversal doesn't escape the cache (SHA-of-anything is a fixed 64-hex) but the audit file now records a misleading canonical URL that suggests a path traversal was attempted -- making forensics harder, not easier.",
      "impact": "Forensic-noise and confusing audit output. A user who pastes `git@host:../../../etc/passwd` sees the audit file record `ssh://git@host/../../../etc/passwd.git` verbatim. In strict environments this could trip security scanners looking at cache contents. The user-facing error from the subsequent git clone will be opaque.",
      "recommendation": "Tighten scpPattern path group to disallow subsequent colons AND disallow a leading `..` segment (or leading `/` which is already stripped). Reject the whole input at Canonicalize stage if the path contains `../` tokens or starts with `/`. Add unsupported.txt fixtures for these cases.",
      "owner": "human",
      "autofix_class": "advisory"
    },
    {
      "id": "adv-007",
      "title": "Composition: StopAt pointing outside the cwd's ancestor chain is silently ignored",
      "severity": "medium",
      "confidence": 0.95,
      "category": "contract-mismatch",
      "file": "internal/workspace/discover.go",
      "lines": "37-77",
      "evidence_constructed_scenario": "Setup: `tmp/a/.aienv.yaml` exists, cwd=`tmp/a/b`, Options.StopAt=`tmp/unrelated` (an existing directory that is NOT on the ancestor chain of cwd). Find walks `tmp/a/b` -> not-found, checks stopAbs == dir (they're unequal), `tmp/a` -> finds manifest, returns it successfully. The intended invariant ('discovery refuses to look above StopAt') is silently violated because the loop only terminates when `dir == stopAbs` exactly. Verified: test `TestAdv_StopAtOutsideAncestorChain` returned a valid Workspace with Root=`tmp/a` even though StopAt pointed elsewhere. The user thought they had bounded the search; they hadn't. This is R8 territory ('logical discovery, cycle-safe walk') and the plan says `AIENVS_WORKSPACE_STOP_AT` is user-facing.",
      "impact": "A user sets `AIENVS_WORKSPACE_STOP_AT=/home/alice/projects` (intending to block discovery from escaping their projects dir), but an invocation from `/tmp/foo/bar` silently walks all the way to `/` looking for a manifest, because `/home/alice/projects` is not an ancestor of `/tmp/foo/bar`. An unintended `.aienv.yaml` in `/tmp` (or worse, `/`) is picked up. The security property the env var claims to provide (bounded upward walk) is only enforced when the operator happened to have the right ancestor -- i.e., when the var is redundant.",
      "recommendation": "Compute stopAbs as an ancestor-check: before the walk, require `strings.HasPrefix(cwdAbs+string(os.PathSeparator), stopAbs+string(os.PathSeparator)) || cwdAbs == stopAbs`. If stopAbs is not an ancestor of cwd, return ErrInvalidOptions with a clear message naming both paths. Or change semantics so StopAt acts as an upper-bound ancestor (walk terminates if dir would become a strict ancestor of stopAbs). Document the chosen semantics in the Options godoc and add a test covering the 'outside the chain' case.",
      "owner": "human",
      "autofix_class": "advisory"
    },
    {
      "id": "adv-008",
      "title": "Abuse: .aienv.yaml present as a directory silently shadowed by ancestor manifest",
      "severity": "medium",
      "confidence": 0.95,
      "category": "assumption-violation",
      "file": "internal/workspace/discover.go",
      "lines": "128-141",
      "evidence_constructed_scenario": "Setup: `tmp/.aienv.yaml` (regular file, valid), `tmp/workspace/.aienv.yaml` (a DIRECTORY, whether user error or malicious crafting). Find(`tmp/workspace`) iterates: regularFileExists returns false for the directory at `tmp/workspace/.aienv.yaml` because `fi.Mode().IsRegular()` is false, so discovery walks past it and returns the ancestor manifest at `tmp`. No warning. Verified via TestAdv_ManifestIsADirectory.",
      "impact": "A user who intended a local workspace but accidentally created `.aienv.yaml` as a directory (e.g., unpacking a tarball, rsync over a mid-edit state, git checkout across a rename-typechange) gets their parent's workspace bound instead. Subsequent writes via fsroot will land in the parent, contaminating siblings. The symlink case (manifest-is-a-symlink-to-outside) is similarly silent: Find succeeds, Workspace.Root is the symlink dir but the manifest bytes come from outside the Root -- fsroot's containment check then clashes with expectations.",
      "recommendation": "If `.aienv.yaml` exists at `<dir>` but is not a regular file (is a directory, device, socket, or dangling symlink), STOP the walk and return a clear error naming the node. Do not silently continue upward. Symlinked manifest pointing outside Root should either be followed (current behavior) WITH a warning to the logger, or rejected; pick one and document it. This is also a hardening point for R8.",
      "owner": "human",
      "autofix_class": "advisory"
    },
    {
      "id": "adv-009",
      "title": "Abuse: anchor alias in canonical.url silently rewrites canonical.ref to the same URL",
      "severity": "low",
      "confidence": 0.85,

      "file": "internal/manifest/load.go",
      "lines": "37-46",
      "evidence_constructed_scenario": "Input: `canonical:\\n  url: &a https://example.com/x.git\\n  ref: *a\\n`. goccy expands *a to the URL string. Load succeeds. m.Canonical.Ref == `https://example.com/x.git` (the URL). No anchor-expansion-depth bound; goccy may or may not have billion-laughs hardening (not verified, but a 10MB ref string loads in 160ms so large inputs are accepted without bound). The explicit `anchors:` top-level key IS rejected as 'unknown field' (verified), but inline anchors inside known fields are permitted and expanded.",

An adversarial manifest can make downstream readers believe `ref` is set to something that is actually derived from `url`, which would surface as a weird error only at unit 5 git-clone time. Not a security issue because downstream validation will catch absurd ref names. But combined with 'absurd input produces a cache entry' (adv-005), a supply-chain-targeting manifest can force weird cache states that are hard to unpack during incident response.",
      "recommendation": "Consider gating anchor expansion via goccy options if available, or explicitly document that anchors/aliases are allowed but produce possibly surprising decodes. Add a load-time size cap (e.g., 256KB manifest) -- this is mentioned by the reliability reviewer (rel-003) and aligns.",

      "autofix_class": "advisory"

    {
      "id": "adv-010",
      "title": "Cascade: WriteResolvedSHA fast path bypasses template-key validation",
      "severity": "low",
      "confidence": 0.80,
      "category": "contract-mismatch",
      "file": "internal/manifest/write.go",
      "lines": "50-54",
      "evidence_constructed_scenario": "If both commit and trustedSHA are \"\", WriteResolvedSHA returns `copy(orig)` WITHOUT parsing, without touching the AST, without checking that the target keys exist. The documented contract (write.go:38-40, spec manifest-v1.md) says 'Both target keys MUST already exist in orig ... If either key is missing, the function returns ErrKeyMissing'. That contract is silently not enforced on the no-op path. Verified indirectly by the existing TestWriteResolvedSHA_NoOpReturnsCopy. The caller pattern that breaks: a future orchestrator that calls WriteResolvedSHA(origOfMissingTemplate, \"\", \"\") believing 'if template keys are missing, this call errors'. It doesn't; it silently succeeds, and the next call (with a real SHA) raises ErrKeyMissing, confusing the state machine.",
      "impact": "No production caller exists yet (unit 5 and unit 6 pass non-empty SHAs), so it's a future-caller trap rather than a current bug. But the invariant 'WriteResolvedSHA(orig, ...) validates orig's keys' is advertised in both godoc and spec; fast path violates it.",
      "recommendation": "Either (a) always parse orig and validate the keys exist, even when both values are empty (cost: one extra parse on a no-op that is rare), or (b) rename the fast path and document it as 'passthrough; does not validate'. Recommend (a): the parse is cheap and the contract is easier to reason about.",
      "owner": "human",
      "autofix_class": "advisory"
    },
    {
      "id": "adv-011",
      "title": "Plan contradiction: Unit 14 marked 'MVP excludes' but plan claims orphan+adopt ship in MVP with Unit 13",
      "severity": "high",
      "confidence": 0.95,
      "category": "plan-inconsistency",
      "file": "docs/plans/2026-04-21-001-feat-aienvs-workspace-cli-plan.md",
      "lines": "1393, 1007-1019",
      "evidence_constructed_scenario": "Line 1393: 'MVP excludes: Units 8b, 10, 11, 14 (orphan + adopt ship in MVP with Unit 13; *adopt-prefix UX wizard* defers), 17, ...'. But Unit 13's Files section (lines 943-953) includes NO orphan/adopt/drift files. Unit 14's Files (lines 1016-1020) EXCLUSIVELY owns `internal/sync/orphans.go`, `internal/sync/adopt.go`, `internal/sync/drift.go`, `internal/cli/cmd_adopt.go`. The plan's decision #12 (line 129) says 'Mid-life drift uses the same guard' and asserts the invariant 'ledger-or-adopt holds for the entire lifetime of the workspace.' That invariant is implemented in Unit 14's drift.go. If Unit 14 is excluded from MVP, then orphan deletion, adoption, AND mid-life drift are all out of MVP -- but the parenthetical says the opposite. Three mutually-contradictory claims in one line.",
      "impact": "When the team starts implementing MVP, whoever picks up Unit 13 will build atomic swap without orphan deletion (because those files aren't in Unit 13's Files list) and without mid-life drift (because that's Unit 14's drift.go). The first sync that runs will succeed; the second sync will not delete orphans; a mid-life drift will not be detected. The R10 claim 'reserved prefix is a single-owner space' is silently broken from day one. This burns implementation time: implementer either (a) follows the Files list and ships a vulnerable MVP, (b) follows the parenthetical and ad-hoc imports Unit 14 files into Unit 13's scope, violating the per-unit seam the plan establishes. Either choice creates cleanup work.",
      "recommendation": "Fix the contradiction explicitly. Recommended resolution: 'MVP includes Unit 14 core (orphans.go + drift.go); defers Unit 14 adopt-prefix wizard.' Update line 1383 to list Unit 14 in MVP includes with a scope-subset note, and update Unit 14's files section to tag which files are MVP and which are v0.2. Alternative: MVP excludes Unit 14 entirely AND the claim about orphan+adopt shipping in MVP is removed -- but then decision #12's 'entire lifetime' invariant needs a weakening that's honest about MVP's limitations.",
      "owner": "human",
      "autofix_class": "advisory"
    },
    {
      "id": "adv-012",
      "title": "Plan gap: Unit 12a is MVP-required but its consumer adapters (cursor, codex) are post-MVP, leaving only an integration test for 12a to prove correctness",

      "file": "docs/plans/2026-04-21-001-feat-aienvs-workspace-cli-plan.md",
      "lines": "1387, 855-933",
      "evidence_constructed_scenario": "MVP includes Unit 12a (lines 855-933: JSON + TOML + markdown-section merge primitives). The spec frames the primary consumers as (a) cursor's .cursor/mcp.json and AGENTS.md, (b) codex's .codex/config.toml and AGENTS.md, (c) claude's .mcp.json. MVP includes only claude (Unit 9). Claude's only tool-owned-file consumer (per Unit 9 approach) is .mcp.json (JSON). So in MVP, Unit 12a's TOML and markdown-section code paths exist but have ZERO production consumer. Combined with the per-external-file lock in Unit 12 ('serializes workspace AGENTS.md written by both cursor + codex adapters') -- neither cursor nor codex ships in MVP, so the lock primitive's only use-case is also deferred.",

(a) is cleaner per Rule of Three. Update the plan's MVP includes line accordingly.",
      "owner": "human",
      "autofix_class": "advisory"

    {
      "id": "adv-013",
      "title": "Abuse: URL-encoded %2F in path decoded to / produces unexpected canonical collisions",
      "severity": "low",
      "confidence": 0.85,
      "category": "boundary-walking",
      "file": "internal/cache/canonicalize.go",
      "lines": "101",
      "evidence_constructed_scenario": "Inputs `https://github.com/Foo%2FBar` and `https://github.com/Foo/Bar` both canonicalize to `https://github.com/Foo/Bar.git` with identical cache keys. net/url.Parse sets u.Path to the decoded form, so `%2F` is semantically a slash after parse. For git URLs, this is probably the right behavior (git doesn't distinguish), but for operators auditing manifests the distinction is lost. Separately, `https://github.com/foo/bar%00.git` (null byte in path) is rejected by net/url.Parse with 'invalid control character in URL' -- good. Newline (`\\x0a`) and tab in path also rejected. But embedded `\\xff` (non-UTF-8 byte) in path is accepted by url.Parse and becomes `%FF` after canonicalization. No rejection of arbitrary 8-bit bytes in path.",
      "impact": "Low. Any two URLs that differ only in their %-encoding of path characters converge to one cache key, which is arguably correct. The non-UTF-8 byte case is more concerning: `https://example.com/\\xff/x` canonicalizes to `https://example.com/%FF/x.git` and hashes successfully. A git clone will likely fail, but the cache directory gets created. No security impact (hash truncates to hex), but another form of audit-file pollution if an adversarial manifest contains 8-bit-binary in canonical.url.",
      "recommendation": "Require canonical.url to be valid ASCII + printable UTF-8 (reject bytes outside the printable ASCII range + Unicode letters). Or accept them and document the canonicalization's %-encoding behavior so audit readers aren't surprised.",
      "owner": "human",
      "autofix_class": "advisory"
    },
    {
      "id": "adv-014",
      "title": "Abuse: IPv6 bracket-host with default port silently becomes unbracketed in canonical form",
      "severity": "medium",
      "confidence": 0.90,
      "category": "assumption-violation",
      "file": "internal/cache/canonicalize.go",
      "lines": "77-85",
      "evidence_constructed_scenario": "Input `https://[::1]:443/foo/bar`. `u.Hostname()` returns `::1` (no brackets). The code then sets `u.Host = host` (no brackets, just `::1`) because 443 is the default port for https and is stripped. u.String() produces `https://::1/foo/bar.git`. Verified. This string is not a valid URL by RFC 3986 (IPv6 literals must be bracketed), and re-parsing it would fail. A canonical form that doesn't round-trip through url.Parse is a latent bug for any downstream tool that re-parses. Also, for the NON-default-port case `https://[::1]:8080/foo/bar`, line 82 does `host + \":\" + port` which produces `::1:8080` (again unbracketed), also invalid.",
      "impact": "Any IPv6-host Git URL (rare for public repos but common for internal dev infra) canonicalizes to a form that re-parse cannot handle. Audit file records an invalid URL. If any downstream consumer (Unit 5's git gateway, Unit 6's trust-store) treats canonical URL as re-parseable, it will fail. Test coverage includes no IPv6 case, so this is invisible until deployment.",
      "recommendation": "Rebracket IPv6 hostnames when assigning u.Host: if host contains ':' it's a literal IPv6 address and must be wrapped in brackets in u.Host. Fix: `if strings.Contains(host, \":\") { host = \"[\" + host + \"]\" }` before the u.Host assignment. Add golden fixtures: `https://[::1]/foo/bar` -> `https://[::1]/foo/bar.git`; `https://[::1]:443/foo/bar` -> `https://[::1]/foo/bar.git`; `https://[::1]:8080/foo/bar` -> `https://[::1]:8080/foo/bar.git`.",
      "owner": "human",
      "autofix_class": "advisory"
    },
    {
      "id": "adv-015",
      "title": "Cascade: WriteAudit overwrite on a shared cache key allows one workspace to clobber another's audit record",
      "severity": "medium",
      "confidence": 0.80,
      "category": "shared-state-mutation",
      "file": "internal/cache/location.go",
      "lines": "94-102",
      "evidence_constructed_scenario": "Two workspaces W1 and W2 on the same machine both resolve the same canonical URL (U1). Both compute the same key K = Key(U1). Both Resolve() to the same Dir and AuditPath. W1 calls WriteAudit() with its Location{Canonical: U1_W1_copy}; W2 then calls WriteAudit() with Location{Canonical: U1_W2_copy}. Since the audit path is shared and WriteAudit uses os.WriteFile with O_TRUNC, W2's content overwrites W1's. If W1 and W2 came from distinct raw URLs that canonicalized identically, their audit files differ only in the raw form (which isn't stored). But: an adversarial workspace that holds a shared cache root (e.g., `/opt/aienvs-cache` passed as Override via both manifests) can craft a Location manually with a DIFFERENT Canonical than the key's real pre-image and call WriteAudit() -- overwriting the audit record for a victim workspace's key with a lie. Verified via test that constructs a Location with a forged Canonical and WriteAudit overwrites successfully.",
      "impact": "For a shared-cache scenario (e.g., a build agent running many repo syncs under one cache root), audit integrity is not preserved. An incident response team looking at `canonical-url.txt` after a suspicious sync could be misled about what URL the cache entry corresponds to. Mitigating factor: the cache key SHA is irreversible, so the audit file is the ONLY human-readable source of truth -- which makes its forgeability a real problem. The plan's statement 'safe to write into an audit trail (no secrets)' is true but the audit trail itself is not integrity-protected.",
      "recommendation": "Either (a) require WriteAudit to re-verify that Location.Canonical hashes to Location.Key before writing (cheap, 1 line), (b) use O_EXCL|O_CREATE and tolerate 'already exists' as success-no-op when content matches (read-verify-skip), or (c) document explicitly that audit is best-effort and not an integrity record. (a) is the strongest: it prevents forged-Canonical writes outright.",
      "owner": "human",
      "autofix_class": "advisory"
    }

Downstream consumers that interpret canonical URL as a git-remote URL for clone will error; consumers that use it as a display string may mislead users.",

    }

    {
      "id": "tg-001",

      "suggested_test": "Add to testdata/urls/golden.txt: `https://[::1]/foo/bar` -> `https://[::1]/foo/bar.git`; `https://[::1]:443/foo/bar` -> `https://[::1]/foo/bar.git`; `https://[::1]:8080/foo/bar` -> `https://[::1]:8080/foo/bar.git`."
    },
    {
      "id": "tg-002",
      "description": "No manifest write test covers CRLF input. adv-002's silent line-ending conversion is invisible.",

    },

      "description": "No test asserts that after Validate(), m.Canonical.Commit/m.TrustedSHA contain trimmed values. The TrimSpace happens in a local variable only.",
      "suggested_test": "Add a load test: give manifest with ` commit: \"1111...1111 \" `, assert err=nil AND m.Canonical.Commit == \"1111...1111\" (no whitespace). Fails today per adv-001."
    },
    {
      "id": "tg-006",
      "description": "No test verifies WriteAudit integrity (that Canonical hashes to Key).",
      "suggested_test": "Construct Location with Canonical=X and Key=Key(Y) where X!=Y; call WriteAudit; assert either error or that the written content matches what Canonicalize(X) would produce (not a forged string)."
    }
  ]

2	  "reviewer": "maintainability",
3	  "findings": [
4	    {
5	      "id": "mtn-1",
6	      "title": "TestKeyPrefix_Participates is a stub — comment promises a check the body does not perform",
7	      "severity": "medium",
8	      "file": "/Users/justinbeaudry/Projects/aienvs/internal/cache/key_test.go",
9	      "line": 40,
10	      "confidence": 0.92,
11	      "autofix_class": "safe-mechanical",
12	      "owner": "author",
13	      "requires_verification": false,
14	      "pre_existing": false,
15	      "category": "half-finished code",
16	      "detail": "The function's multi-line doc comment describes a verification it never performs: 'confirm by hashing \"git:<url>\" manually and checking that Key matches that, not the bare URL' and 'showing two different prefixes produce different keys'. The body only asserts that the hex output contains no space character — trivially true for any crypto/sha256 hex string. The test name and comment suggest the prefix is load-bearing, but a reviewer changing KeyPrefix could break cache-key stability and every test in the package would still pass. Either compute sha256 of the prefix+URL manually and assert equality, or delete the test. As-is it imposes a maintenance liability (looks meaningful, isn't).",
17	      "suggested_fix": "Replace body with: expected := hex.EncodeToString(sha256.Sum256([]byte(\"git:https://github.com/foo/bar.git\"))[:]); if cache.Key(\"https://github.com/foo/bar.git\") != expected { t.Errorf(...) }. Alternatively, drop the test; equivalence/golden tests already lock cache-key stability."
18	    },
19	    {
20	      "id": "mtn-2",
21	      "title": "WriteTrustedSHA is a single-call passthrough around WriteResolvedSHA",
22	      "severity": "low",
23	      "file": "/Users/justinbeaudry/Projects/aienvs/internal/manifest/write.go",
24	      "line": 84,
25	      "confidence": 0.6,
26	      "autofix_class": "needs-human",
27	      "owner": "author",
28	      "requires_verification": false,
29	      "pre_existing": false,
30	      "category": "premature abstraction / indirection",
31	      "detail": "func WriteTrustedSHA(orig, trustedSHA) is a one-liner that calls WriteResolvedSHA(orig, \"\", trustedSHA). It exists to give the `aienvs trust pin` command a named symbol, but the trust-pin handler lives in a later unit and doesn't exist yet. Per CLAUDE.md's 'do not abstract before the third duplication' rule, the wrapper is speculative. Judgment call because the name does document intent. Acceptable to keep if the trust handler lands in the same sprint; worth deleting if it sits unused beyond Unit 5.",
32	      "suggested_fix": "Either (a) inline by calling WriteResolvedSHA(orig, \"\", sha) at the one caller when it exists, or (b) keep with a // USED BY: comment pointing to the unit that will consume it, so the next reader understands the intent."
33	    },
34	    {
35	      "id": "mtn-3",
36	      "title": "Cache package re-exports ErrUnsupportedURL from canonicalize.go for a validation in location.go",
37	      "severity": "low",
38	      "file": "/Users/justinbeaudry/Projects/aienvs/internal/cache/location.go",
39	      "line": 58,
40	      "confidence": 0.55,
41	      "autofix_class": "safe-mechanical",
42	      "owner": "author",
43	      "requires_verification": false,
44	      "pre_existing": false,
45	      "category": "naming that obscures intent",
46	      "detail": "Resolve() returns ErrUnsupportedURL with message 'empty canonical URL'. But ErrUnsupportedURL is defined in canonicalize.go specifically for 'URL is syntactically unparseable or uses a scheme aienvs does not support'. An empty string passed directly to Resolve (skipping Canonicalize) isn't really a scheme/parsing problem — it's a precondition violation. Reusing the sentinel couples two unrelated failure modes behind one error. A future caller adding errors.Is(err, ErrUnsupportedURL) to distinguish URL errors from other cache errors will be surprised that 'I passed an empty canonical' is bucketed as 'unsupported scheme'. Minor — but exactly the kind of sentinel coupling CLAUDE.md's sentinel-errors guidance warns against.",
47	      "suggested_fix": "Either introduce a separate sentinel (e.g. ErrInvalidCanonical) or document on the Resolve doc that empty input returns ErrUnsupportedURL as a precondition violation. TestResolve_RejectsEmptyCanonical already locks in the current behavior, so this is a naming/documentation fix, not a behavior change."
48	    },
49	    {
50	      "id": "mtn-4",
51	      "title": "KeyFromRaw and ResolveFromRaw are speculative convenience wrappers with no in-tree callers",
52	      "severity": "low",
53	      "file": "/Users/justinbeaudry/Projects/aienvs/internal/cache/key.go",
54	      "line": 29,
55	      "confidence": 0.72,
56	      "autofix_class": "safe-mechanical",
57	      "owner": "author",
58	      "requires_verification": true,
59	      "pre_existing": false,
60	      "category": "premature abstraction",
61	      "detail": "Both KeyFromRaw (key.go:29) and ResolveFromRaw (location.go:78) are Canonicalize→X pipelines. Neither has any caller under internal/ (this is Unit 4; Units 5/6 haven't landed). The doc for KeyFromRaw even says 'most callers have the canonical form already', which argues *against* exposing the convenience wrapper. Two pipelines for the same two-step operation cost extra test surface and introduce the risk of a caller picking the *FromRaw variant where they should cache the canonical form once and reuse it. Rule of Three is not yet met: one known real caller site at best.",
62	      "suggested_fix": "Drop KeyFromRaw and ResolveFromRaw until the first real consumer appears. If that consumer can simply write two lines (canon, _ := Canonicalize(raw); loc := Resolve(canon, opts)), the wrappers aren't earning their keep. Remove the tests that only cover the wrapper path too."
63	    },
64	    {
65	      "id": "mtn-5",
66	      "title": "DefaultMaxHops=64 is a magic number with no plan-level rationale beyond 'safety net'",
67	      "severity": "low",
68	      "file": "/Users/justinbeaudry/Projects/aienvs/internal/workspace/workspace.go",

Low severity — naming/documentation nit.",
77	      "suggested_fix": "Either cite the plan decision (R8) in the doc comment, or add a one-line comment naming the concrete cycle pathology (e.g. symlink loop creating a cycle the walk treats as ever-deepening). Alternatively: leave as-is; low priority."
78	    },

81	      "title": "LoadFile's inner 'if err != nil; return nil, err' block is pure indirection",
82	      "severity": "low",
83	      "file": "/Users/justinbeaudry/Projects/aienvs/internal/manifest/load.go",
84	      "line": 25,
85	      "confidence": 0.8,
86	      "autofix_class": "safe-mechanical",
87	      "owner": "author",
88	      "requires_verification": false,
89	      "pre_existing": false,

91	      "detail": "LoadFile reads the file, then calls LoadBytes, then does 'if err != nil { return nil, err }; return m, nil'. The second check adds zero value — it's a direct passthrough. Replacing both lines with 'return LoadBytes(b, opts)' is shorter and removes a level of cognitive overhead. Trivial but exactly the kind of structural hygiene CLAUDE.md's SRP/clarity guidance calls out.",
92	      "suggested_fix": "Replace lines 30-34 with: return LoadBytes(b, opts)"
93	    },
94	    {
95	      "id": "mtn-7",
96	      "title": "TestWriteResolvedSHA_NoOpReturnsCopy mutates the returned slice to test aliasing — fragile and reader-hostile",
97	      "severity": "low",
98	      "file": "/Users/justinbeaudry/Projects/aienvs/internal/manifest/write_test.go",
99	      "line": 117,
100	      "confidence": 0.5,
101	      "autofix_class": "safe-mechanical",
102	      "owner": "author",

104	      "pre_existing": false,
105	      "category": "test clarity",
106	      "detail": "The test does 'out[0] = X; if orig[0] == X { fail }' to verify the no-op path returns a copy. This is load-bearing behavior if any caller retains the input slice, but the mutation idiom relies on 'orig' being re-read from disk *inside* readFixture each test invocation, and 'out' being a distinct []byte. A future refactor that caches fixture bytes (e.g. returning a shared []byte from a package-level fixture) would make this test pass incorrectly. Prefer &out[0] != &orig[0] or unsafe.SliceData pointer compare, or assert via len capacity that they are distinct allocations.",
107	      "suggested_fix": "Replace with a pointer-identity check: if len(orig) > 0 && len(out) > 0 && &out[0] == &orig[0] { t.Error(\"output aliases input buffer\") }"
108	    },

121	      "detail": "Manifest.Floating is declared and unmarshaled, but Validate never reads it. The doc says 'Floating controls whether canonical.ref may remain floating at sync time' — yet Validate does no such check. Today a manifest with floating: true and no commit is accepted; a manifest with floating: false and no commit is also accepted. The field is planned for Unit 5. Until then it's a public schema surface with no enforced semantics — users can write floating: true and it has zero effect, which will be a confusing regression when Unit 5 starts enforcing. Flag so Unit 5 is scheduled to close the loop (or remove the field until then).",
122	      "suggested_fix": "Option A: remove the Floating field and its YAML binding until Unit 5 is ready to enforce it. Option B: keep but add a load-time validation warning (or integration test placeholder) tracking a TODO linked to Unit 5. Either way, avoid advertising a knob that silently does nothing."

The doc warns 'callers should not treat it as authoritative for filesystem containment checks — use Root.' If the field exists only for diagnostics, consider whether it needs to be exported — an exported field creates an API contract that is easy to abuse later. Judgment call; Unit 3 may be correctly preparing for the CLI to surface it in `aienvs status` output.",

153	    }

157	    "Cache-key stability depends on (a) Canonicalize rules being frozen and (b) KeyPrefix being frozen. The only test that would catch accidental KeyPrefix drift is TestKeyPrefix_Participates, which as written does not actually verify the prefix participates in the hash (finding mtn-1). Golden + equivalence tests do pin canonicalization output, but they do not catch a KeyPrefix change since they only exercise cache.Key through the same prefix on both sides.",

A symlink loop on a deeply nested filesystem still terminates, but the exact pathology it mitigates is not documented. If someone lowers this constant for perf later, they may re-open a pathological-path class without realizing it.",

If the manifest lives at the root of a real filesystem (pathological but possible in tests / containers), filepath.Dir returns the same path — safe — but the coupling between WriteFile and fsroot's containment semantics is implicit. A future refactor that moves WriteFile to accept a *fsroot.Root directly would be cleaner and remove the Abs/Dir dance."

The round-trip of WriteResolvedSHA(orig, newCommit, \"\") → LoadBytes NonInteractive path is not exercised, so the CI guard around non-interactive + resolved-commit-but-no-trusted could regress without a failing test.",

file.content:
{
  "reviewer": "maintainability",
  "findings": [
    {
      "id": "mtn-1",
      "title": "TestKeyPrefix_Participates is a stub — comment promises a check the body does not perform",
      "severity": "medium",
      "file": "/Users/justinbeaudry/Projects/aienvs/internal/cache/key_test.go",
      "line": 40,
      "confidence": 0.92,
      "autofix_class": "safe-mechanical",
      "owner": "author",
      "requires_verification": false,
      "pre_existing": false,
      "category": "half-finished code",
      "detail": "The function's multi-line doc comment describes a verification it never performs: 'confirm by hashing \"git:<url>\" manually and checking that Key matches that, not the bare URL' and 'showing two different prefixes produce different keys'. The body only asserts that the hex output contains no space character — trivially true for any crypto/sha256 hex string. The test name and comment suggest the prefix is load-bearing, but a reviewer changing KeyPrefix could break cache-key stability and every test in the package would still pass. Either compute sha256 of the prefix+URL manually and assert equality, or delete the test. As-is it imposes a maintenance liability (looks meaningful, isn't).",
      "suggested_fix": "Replace body with: expected := hex.EncodeToString(sha256.Sum256([]byte(\"git:https://github.com/foo/bar.git\"))[:]); if cache.Key(\"https://github.com/foo/bar.git\") != expected { t.Errorf(...) }. Alternatively, drop the test; equivalence/golden tests already lock cache-key stability."
    },
    {
      "id": "mtn-2",
      "title": "WriteTrustedSHA is a single-call passthrough around WriteResolvedSHA",
      "severity": "low",
      "file": "/Users/justinbeaudry/Projects/aienvs/internal/manifest/write.go",
      "line": 84,
      "confidence": 0.6,
      "autofix_class": "needs-human",
      "owner": "author",
      "requires_verification": false,
      "pre_existing": false,
      "category": "premature abstraction / indirection",
      "detail": "func WriteTrustedSHA(orig, trustedSHA) is a one-liner that calls WriteResolvedSHA(orig, \"\", trustedSHA). It exists to give the `aienvs trust pin` command a named symbol, but the trust-pin handler lives in a later unit and doesn't exist yet. Per CLAUDE.md's 'do not abstract before the third duplication' rule, the wrapper is speculative. Judgment call because the name does document intent. Acceptable to keep if the trust handler lands in the same sprint; worth deleting if it sits unused beyond Unit 5.",
      "suggested_fix": "Either (a) inline by calling WriteResolvedSHA(orig, \"\", sha) at the one caller when it exists, or (b) keep with a // USED BY: comment pointing to the unit that will consume it, so the next reader understands the intent."
    },
    {
      "id": "mtn-3",
      "title": "Cache package re-exports ErrUnsupportedURL from canonicalize.go for a validation in location.go",
      "severity": "low",
      "file": "/Users/justinbeaudry/Projects/aienvs/internal/cache/location.go",
      "line": 58,
      "confidence": 0.55,
      "autofix_class": "safe-mechanical",
      "owner": "author",
      "requires_verification": false,
      "pre_existing": false,
      "category": "naming that obscures intent",
      "detail": "Resolve() returns ErrUnsupportedURL with message 'empty canonical URL'. But ErrUnsupportedURL is defined in canonicalize.go specifically for 'URL is syntactically unparseable or uses a scheme aienvs does not support'. An empty string passed directly to Resolve (skipping Canonicalize) isn't really a scheme/parsing problem — it's a precondition violation. Reusing the sentinel couples two unrelated failure modes behind one error. A future caller adding errors.Is(err, ErrUnsupportedURL) to distinguish URL errors from other cache errors will be surprised that 'I passed an empty canonical' is bucketed as 'unsupported scheme'. Minor — but exactly the kind of sentinel coupling CLAUDE.md's sentinel-errors guidance warns against.",
      "suggested_fix": "Either introduce a separate sentinel (e.g. ErrInvalidCanonical) or document on the Resolve doc that empty input returns ErrUnsupportedURL as a precondition violation. TestResolve_RejectsEmptyCanonical already locks in the current behavior, so this is a naming/documentation fix, not a behavior change."
    },
    {
      "id": "mtn-4",
      "title": "KeyFromRaw and ResolveFromRaw are speculative convenience wrappers with no in-tree callers",
      "severity": "low",
      "file": "/Users/justinbeaudry/Projects/aienvs/internal/cache/key.go",
      "line": 29,
      "confidence": 0.72,
      "autofix_class": "safe-mechanical",
      "owner": "author",
      "requires_verification": true,
      "pre_existing": false,
      "category": "premature abstraction",
      "detail": "Both KeyFromRaw (key.go:29) and ResolveFromRaw (location.go:78) are Canonicalize→X pipelines. Neither has any caller under internal/ (this is Unit 4; Units 5/6 haven't landed). The doc for KeyFromRaw even says 'most callers have the canonical form already', which argues *against* exposing the convenience wrapper. Two pipelines for the same two-step operation cost extra test surface and introduce the risk of a caller picking the *FromRaw variant where they should cache the canonical form once and reuse it. Rule of Three is not yet met: one known real caller site at best.",
      "suggested_fix": "Drop KeyFromRaw and ResolveFromRaw until the first real consumer appears. If that consumer can simply write two lines (canon, _ := Canonicalize(raw); loc := Resolve(canon, opts)), the wrappers aren't earning their keep. Remove the tests that only cover the wrapper path too."
    },
    {
      "id": "mtn-5",

      "owner": "author",

Low severity — naming/documentation nit.",

    },

      "title": "LoadFile's inner 'if err != nil; return nil, err' block is pure indirection",

      "line": 25,
      "confidence": 0.8,
      "autofix_class": "safe-mechanical",
      "owner": "author",
      "requires_verification": false,
      "pre_existing": false,
      "category": "unnecessary indirection",
      "detail": "LoadFile reads the file, then calls LoadBytes, then does 'if err != nil { return nil, err }; return m, nil'. The second check adds zero value — it's a direct passthrough. Replacing both lines with 'return LoadBytes(b, opts)' is shorter and removes a level of cognitive overhead. Trivial but exactly the kind of structural hygiene CLAUDE.md's SRP/clarity guidance calls out.",
      "suggested_fix": "Replace lines 30-34 with: return LoadBytes(b, opts)"
    },
    {
      "id": "mtn-7",
      "title": "TestWriteResolvedSHA_NoOpReturnsCopy mutates the returned slice to test aliasing — fragile and reader-hostile",
      "severity": "low",
      "file": "/Users/justinbeaudry/Projects/aienvs/internal/manifest/write_test.go",
      "line": 117,
      "confidence": 0.5,
      "autofix_class": "safe-mechanical",
      "owner": "author",
      "requires_verification": false,
      "pre_existing": false,
      "category": "test clarity",
      "detail": "The test does 'out[0] = X; if orig[0] == X { fail }' to verify the no-op path returns a copy. This is load-bearing behavior if any caller retains the input slice, but the mutation idiom relies on 'orig' being re-read from disk *inside* readFixture each test invocation, and 'out' being a distinct []byte. A future refactor that caches fixture bytes (e.g. returning a shared []byte from a package-level fixture) would make this test pass incorrectly. Prefer &out[0] != &orig[0] or unsafe.SliceData pointer compare, or assert via len capacity that they are distinct allocations.",
      "suggested_fix": "Replace with a pointer-identity check: if len(orig) > 0 && len(out) > 0 && &out[0] == &orig[0] { t.Error(\"output aliases input buffer\") }"
    },
    {
      "id": "mtn-8",
      "title": "Floating field is declared in schema but never read by Load/Validate",
      "severity": "low",
      "file": "/Users/justinbeaudry/Projects/aienvs/internal/manifest/schema.go",
      "line": 16,
      "confidence": 0.7,
      "autofix_class": "needs-human",
      "owner": "author",
      "requires_verification": true,
      "pre_existing": false,
      "category": "half-finished code / dead field",
      "detail": "Manifest.Floating is declared and unmarshaled, but Validate never reads it. The doc says 'Floating controls whether canonical.ref may remain floating at sync time' — yet Validate does no such check. Today a manifest with floating: true and no commit is accepted; a manifest with floating: false and no commit is also accepted. The field is planned for Unit 5. Until then it's a public schema surface with no enforced semantics — users can write floating: true and it has zero effect, which will be a confusing regression when Unit 5 starts enforcing. Flag so Unit 5 is scheduled to close the loop (or remove the field until then).",
      "suggested_fix": "Option A: remove the Floating field and its YAML binding until Unit 5 is ready to enforce it. Option B: keep but add a load-time validation warning (or integration test placeholder) tracking a TODO linked to Unit 5. Either way, avoid advertising a knob that silently does nothing."

    {

      "title": "AdapterDecl fields Pin and TrustedSHA are declared 'Reserved for later units' with no consumer",

      "detail": "AdapterDecl.Pin and AdapterDecl.TrustedSHA are explicitly 'Reserved for later units' in their doc comments — same concern as TrustConfig.RequireAttestation and TrustConfig.AllowNewSHAsUntil (schema.go:70-78). Declaring them now bakes the wire format into v1 before their semantics are nailed down. If any of these have to change shape once the consuming unit is written (e.g., TrustedSHA becomes a struct to carry a reason), it's a breaking schema change. Strict schemas (DisallowUnknownField) should be especially conservative about speculative keys.",
      "suggested_fix": "Defer these fields to the unit that actually uses them. Since the loader already supports x-* forward-compat extension keys, users who want to experiment can stash values there. When Unit 8/9 lands the real meaning, add the field and a migration note."

If the field exists only for diagnostics, consider whether it needs to be exported — an exported field creates an API contract that is easy to abuse later. Judgment call; Unit 3 may be correctly preparing for the CLI to surface it in `aienvs status` output.",

    }
  ],
  "residual_risks": [

The only test that would catch accidental KeyPrefix drift is TestKeyPrefix_Participates, which as written does not actually verify the prefix participates in the hash (finding mtn-1). Golden + equivalence tests do pin canonicalization output, but they do not catch a KeyPrefix change since they only exercise cache.Key through the same prefix on both sides.",

A symlink loop on a deeply nested filesystem still terminates, but the exact pathology it mitigates is not documented. If someone lowers this constant for perf later, they may re-open a pathological-path class without realizing it.",
    "manifest.WriteFile uses fsroot.OpenWorkspaceRoot on the *parent directory* of the manifest. If the manifest lives at the root of a real filesystem (pathological but possible in tests / containers), filepath.Dir returns the same path — safe — but the coupling between WriteFile and fsroot's containment semantics is implicit. A future refactor that moves WriteFile to accept a *fsroot.Root directly would be cleaner and remove the Abs/Dir dance."
  ],
  "testing_gaps": [
    "No test asserts that Manifest.Floating is actually honored. Either the field is inert (in which case tests should say so via a TestFloatingIsIgnoredInUnit2 placeholder) or its semantics are TBD and the field should not be exported yet.",

The round-trip of WriteResolvedSHA(orig, newCommit, \"\") → LoadBytes NonInteractive path is not exercised, so the CI guard around non-interactive + resolved-commit-but-no-trusted could regress without a failing test.",

content.content:
1	{
2	  "reviewer": "project-standards",
3	  "summary": "Phase A (units 2-4) largely complies with AGENTS.md invariants and the repo style guide. Production writes inside a workspace correctly funnel through fsroot (manifest.WriteFile -> fsroot.OpenWorkspaceRoot + StagedWrite). Tests use testing.T.TempDir and real filesystem as required. Findings concentrate on three style/invariant edges: (1) cache audit-file write uses os.MkdirAll + os.WriteFile directly on a user-owned path, which conflicts with the Style rule 'Use os.Root whenever touching user paths' even though it is consistent with AGENTS.md invariant #1 (which scopes to *workspace* writes); the code documents the opt-out inline. (2) A handful of bare, non-sentinel, non-%w-wrapped errors in internal helpers that the Style rule discourages. (3) manifest.LoadFile returns os.ReadFile errors unwrapped. No hard-invariant breaches (no os.WriteFile/os.Create/os.Rename against workspace paths; Go 1.25 in go.mod; fsroot tests use real FS).",
4	  "findings": [
5	    {
6	      "id": "ps-001",
7	      "title": "Cache audit-file write uses os.MkdirAll + os.WriteFile on user-owned paths instead of os.Root",
8	      "severity": "medium",
9	      "confidence": 0.68,
10	      "where": "internal/cache/location.go:94-102 (Location.WriteAudit)",
11	      "rule": "AGENTS.md Style: 'Go 1.25+. Use os.Root whenever touching user paths.' The cache root resolves under xdg.CacheHome or an absolute override, both of which are user paths.",
12	      "violation": "WriteAudit calls os.MkdirAll(l.Dir, 0o750) and os.WriteFile(l.AuditPath, ...) directly. The doc comment at line 93-95 opts out explicitly ('The write goes through os.WriteFile (not fsroot) because the cache directory is outside the workspace'). AGENTS.md invariant #1 is indeed scoped to *workspace* writes, so this is not an invariant breach; but the broader Style rule applies to *any* user path. The cache dir is a user path by definition.",
13	      "why_it_matters": "Bare os.WriteFile on XDG paths bypasses the symlink/reparse-point defense-in-depth fsroot provides. A malicious symlink at <cache>/<key>/canonical-url.txt could redirect the audit write. Low real-world probability (user would have to plant it in their own cache), but the Style rule exists precisely for this kind of defense-in-depth.",
14	      "remediation": "Either (a) open an os.Root on the cache-entry directory and use a minimal StagedWrite-equivalent, or (b) add an explicit AGENTS.md clarification that the os.Root rule applies only to workspace paths and cache is exempt, and expand the inline comment to reference that clarification. Prefer (a): the fsroot.OpenWorkspaceRoot helper already refuses non-directory targets, and a cache-scoped variant would extend coverage with minimal code.",
15	      "pre_existing": false
16	    },
17	    {
18	      "id": "ps-002",
19	      "title": "manifest.LoadFile returns os.ReadFile error without %w-wrap",
20	      "severity": "low",
21	      "confidence": 0.78,
22	      "where": "internal/manifest/load.go:25-34 (LoadFile)",
23	      "rule": "AGENTS.md Style: 'Errors: wrap with %w, define sentinel errors at package level for any condition a caller is expected to branch on.' Global CLAUDE.md Go Standards: 'Wrap with context: fmt.Errorf(\"doing X: %w\", err).'",
24	      "violation": "Line 28: `return nil, err` returns the raw os.ReadFile error with no context. LoadBytes correctly wraps its failures with %w: ErrInvalidManifest, but LoadFile's path-read failure is passed through bare. A caller doing `errors.Is(err, manifest.ErrInvalidManifest)` will miss the I/O error (it's not a manifest-invalid condition, so that may be intentional), but they also get no 'loading manifest <path>' context string.",
25	      "why_it_matters": "Log lines will show 'open /path/.aienv.yaml: no such file or directory' with no hint that this is an aienvs manifest load failure. Minor UX and debuggability issue.",
26	      "remediation": "`return nil, fmt.Errorf(\"manifest: read %q: %w\", path, err)`. The err still satisfies errors.Is(err, fs.ErrNotExist) so callers can branch on file-not-found.",
27	      "pre_existing": false
28	    },
29	    {
30	      "id": "ps-003",
31	      "title": "absLogical returns bare os.Getwd error without wrap",
32	      "severity": "low",
33	      "confidence": 0.72,
34	      "where": "internal/workspace/discover.go:114-126 (absLogical)",
35	      "rule": "AGENTS.md Style: 'Errors: wrap with %w [...]. Don't return bare fmt.Errorf for conditions callers need to handle programmatically.'",
36	      "violation": "Line 123: `return \"\", err` passes through os.Getwd's error unwrapped. Line 116: `fmt.Errorf(\"empty path\")` returns a bare fmt.Errorf with no sentinel. Callers (Find, resolveExplicit) re-wrap these when they surface them, so the behavior is fine, but inside-the-package the idiom diverges from the repo rule.",
37	      "why_it_matters": "Callers of absLogical always immediately wrap, so externally this is invisible. The concern is consistency — other helpers in the same file (regularFileExists) also return bare errors, setting a pattern that will leak if absLogical is ever called from a new caller that forgets to wrap.",
38	      "remediation": "`return \"\", fmt.Errorf(\"getwd: %w\", err)` and either delete the 'empty path' branch (callers already guard) or define ErrEmptyPath if it's worth branching on.",
39	      "pre_existing": false
40	    },
41	    {
42	      "id": "ps-004",
43	      "title": "Validate returns several distinct conditions as bare fmt.Errorf instead of per-condition sentinels",
44	      "severity": "low",
45	      "confidence": 0.45,
46	      "where": "internal/manifest/load.go:56-106 (Validate)",
47	      "rule": "AGENTS.md Style: 'define sentinel errors at package level for any condition a caller is expected to branch on.'",
48	      "violation": "Validate returns 9 distinct failure modes (bad version, 1:1 violation both-set, 1:1 violation neither-set, trusted-without-commit, bad commit hex, bad trusted hex, non-interactive-missing-trusted, commit/trusted mismatch, bad scope) as plain fmt.Errorf strings. LoadBytes wraps them all under ErrInvalidManifest, which is likely the only class any caller will branch on — so this is probably fine. Flagging at low confidence because a CI integration might reasonably want to branch on, e.g., 'non-interactive missing trusted_sha' specifically to print a different remediation from 'bad hex'.",
49	      "why_it_matters": "If downstream tooling wants to produce different exit codes for different manifest-invalid reasons, it will have to string-match error messages. Minor.",
50	      "remediation": "Optional; defer until a caller actually needs it (Rule of Three). If added, candidates: ErrCanonicalSourceAmbiguous, ErrNonInteractiveRequiresTrustedSHA, ErrCommitMismatch, ErrUnsupportedVersion, ErrInvalidScope.",
51	      "pre_existing": false
52	    },
53	    {
54	      "id": "ps-005",
55	      "title": "Empty-canonical-URL error misclassified as ErrUnsupportedURL",
56	      "severity": "low",
57	      "confidence": 0.55,
58	      "where": "internal/cache/location.go:56-59 (Resolve)",
59	      "rule": "Semantic consistency — AGENTS.md Style implies sentinel errors should name the actual condition; mixing a programming-bug case (empty canonical) with a user-input-class case (unsupported URL) makes errors.Is(err, ErrUnsupportedURL) ambiguous.",
60	      "violation": "Resolve returns `fmt.Errorf(\"%w: empty canonical URL\", ErrUnsupportedURL)` when canonical == \"\". But callers who reach Resolve should have already run Canonicalize, which already rejects empty inputs with ErrUnsupportedURL — so an empty canonical at Resolve time is a programming bug, not an unsupported URL. Conflating the two makes the sentinel less precise.",
61	      "why_it_matters": "Low — the test TestResolve_RejectsEmptyCanonical documents this classification explicitly. But a caller writing `if errors.Is(err, cache.ErrUnsupportedURL) { suggestDifferentUrl() }` would suggest a different URL to the user for what is actually an internal bug.",
62	      "remediation": "Either document this as intentional (a defensive catch-all that should never fire) or introduce ErrEmptyCanonical / return a plain non-sentinel error so misuse surfaces distinctly.",
63	      "pre_existing": false
64	    }
65	  ],
66	  "residual_risks": [
67	    "The global CLAUDE.md Go rule 'Every I/O function takes context.Context as first param' is not enforced in any new signature (manifest.LoadFile, manifest.WriteFile, workspace.Find, cache.Location.WriteAudit). AGENTS.md only requires ctx for 'long-running work', and fsroot's Unit 1 signatures are also context-less, establishing the repo baseline. Future ctx retrofit would be a larger, repo-wide change — out of scope for this review but worth noting when git/network I/O lands in Unit 5."
68	  ],
69	  "testing_gaps": [
70	    "No test in internal/cache exercises WriteAudit against a pre-existing symlink at AuditPath — the os.Root rule in the style guide exists to protect against exactly this.
